text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the When Not to Use AI lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Departments need a clear decision framework for situations where AI is inappropriate, premature, too risky, unsupported by data, legally uncertain, or not worth the operational burden.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Departments need a clear decision framework for situations where AI is inappropriate, premature, too risky, unsupported by data, legally uncertain, or not worth the operational burden.
textPreview: Departments need a clear decision framework for situations where AI is inappropriate, premature, too risky, unsupported by data, legally ...

text: A clear no-go framework protects departments from avoidable harm, unfunded pilots, weak accountability, and loss of trust.
textPreview: A clear no-go framework protects departments from avoidable harm, unfunded pilots, weak accountability, and loss of trust.

text: AI can consume resources, obscure accountability, or create harm when a simpler, safer, human-centered approach is more appropriate.
textPreview: AI can consume resources, obscure accountability, or create harm when a simpler, safer, human-centered approach is more appropriate.

text:
Use this slide in the When Not to Use AI training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 7, 12, 13 and Tools 1, 3, 6, 7, 13, 42, 46.
Emphasize the core guardrail: AI can consume resources, obscure accountability, or create harm when a simpler, safer, human-centered approach is more appropriate.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain When Not to Use AI in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the When Not to Use AI training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 7, 12, 13 and Tools 1, 3, 6, 7, 13, 42, 46.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Responsible AI adoption includes saying no. Some tasks should remain human-only, some require more readiness work before AI is considered, and some can be improved more safely through workflow redesign, staffing, training, or better data systems.

text: Improve the workflow without AI. Strengthen data quality, documentation, staffing, or training first. Use a simpler rules-based tool, checklist, dashboard, or template. Limit AI to internal drafting or evidence organization rather than.

text:
Use this slide in the When Not to Use AI training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 7, 12, 13 and Tools 1, 3, 6, 7, 13, 42, 46.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
Which public health decisions should remain human-only? What conditions would cause governance to reject or defer an AI proposal? What non-AI alternatives could solve the problem more safely?

text: Review three proposed AI use cases and decide whether each should proceed, be limited, be deferred, be redesigned without AI, or be rejected.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: AI no-go decision notes, Restricted-use criteria, Alternative solution options.
